key_findings.finding: Fabric's positioning is 'Consensus Plane for AI-Human Collaboration' — a protocol layer, not a feature playground. Brand promise is correctness/consistency across clients, not breadth of clients.
key_findings.evidence: README.md:9 'The Consensus Plane for AI-Human Collaboration'; 思路.md emphasizes a tool-agnostic AGENTS.md+docs protocol with hard constraints always loaded. Stability IS the product, not a non-functional adjunct.

key_findings.finding: Velocity has been Added-heavy across v1.0→v1.7: every minor adds protocol surface (L0/L1/L2, plan_context, get_rule_sections, ContextCache, Shadow Mirroring, ledger relocation, approve, scan…). No release has been dedicated to 'reduce surface area, harden what exists.' Stabilization debt is real and accumulating.
key_findings.evidence: CHANGELOG.md v1.0→v1.7: 7 minors in ~17 days (2026-04-19 → 2026-04-25), each adding new MCP tools, new metadata shapes, new commands, or new architecture. ac6e17a 'unblock v1.7.0 validation' suggests a release was just pushed past a partial blocker.

key_findings.finding: Of the 6 advertised clients, only 3 (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI) have native first-party assets: skills, hooks, snapshot tests, TOML/JSON differentiated emitters. Windsurf/Roo/Gemini are 'JSON writer + alias + template stub' — shallow support that costs maintenance proportional to a real client without delivering equivalent UX.
key_findings.evidence: exploration-codebase.json kept_clients_dispatch_sites lists hooks+skills+templates ONLY for Claude/Codex/Cursor. Windsurf/Roo/Gemini have NO templates/claude-hooks-equivalent, NO skills, NO writer unit tests (stability_signals.test_coverage_gaps[2]).

key_findings.finding: Schema mismatch + MCP timeouts dominate real-world MCP failures (38% + 24% per industry stress tests). Both are stability concerns we are exposed to, not feature gaps. ROI of stabilization compounds: every fix lands in a smaller, more testable surface.
key_findings.evidence: research.json findings #2 (38% schema mismatches), #1 (24% timeouts). Our own gaps include no contract tests, no MCP handshake conformance test, mixed atomic/non-atomic write semantics on events.jsonl.

key_findings.finding: Windsurf/Roo/Gemini removal is mostly mechanical (15 files, ~200-250 net LoC reduction), bounded by TypeScript's ClientKind union — every consumer is type-checked. Risk of accidental breakage to remaining clients is LOW.
key_findings.evidence: exploration-codebase.json client_scope_map.estimated_removal_files=15; ClientKind union in writer.ts is the typed source of truth (annotation L20-25). TS will flag every dispatch site.

key_findings.finding: fab vs fabric naming inconsistency, three case-variants of client identifiers (rooCode/RooCode/roo, geminiCLI/GeminiCLI/gemini), and two duplicate CLIENT_ALIASES tables are visible polish gaps that cost zero in features but visibly raise the perceived quality bar when fixed.
key_findings.evidence: stability_signals.naming_inconsistencies (3 entries); patterns 'Client alias map duplication' (bootstrap.ts:42-61 vs config.ts:14-33).

key_findings.finding: Pre-v1.0 the user base is necessarily small/early-adopter. The user cohort that will care about Windsurf/Roo/Gemini removal is essentially 'people who tried Fabric in the last 17 days' — a window in which no marketing campaign launched and no public comms specifically promoted those three clients beyond the README sentence.
key_findings.evidence: CHANGELOG entry for v1.0 is dated 2026-04-19; today is 2026-05-06. README.md:11 is the primary surface mentioning all six clients. There is no per-client landing page or marketing artifact for Windsurf/Roo/Gemini.

key_findings.finding: Real product moat is the L0/L1/L2 cognitive protocol + Shadow Mirroring + plan_context handshake — none of which is client-specific. Narrowing client list does NOT narrow the value prop; it focuses validation effort on the clients where the protocol actually demonstrably works end-to-end.
key_findings.evidence: CHANGELOG v1.6.0 introduces L0/L1/L2 protocol; v1.1.0 introduces Shadow Mirroring; both are client-agnostic. Codebase shows skills+hooks+TOML differentiation only for Claude/Codex/Cursor — i.e., the protocol is only end-to-end demonstrated on these three.

stabilization_roi.case_for: Three converging arguments: (1) Brand positioning as a protocol/correctness layer means a single high-profile drift bug damages credibility more than a missing feature; (2) the v1.0→v1.7 sprint ran 7 minors in 17 days adding protocol surface — accumulated, untested seams (resolver, _error.ts string-prefix matching, ledger appendFile vs atomic writes, no MCP handshake conformance test) have a high probability of producing a public incident before next minor; (3) stabilization compounds — every test added becomes regression armor for future feature work, while feature debt today is paid back at 3x interest tomorrow. Industry baseline (research.json) shows MCP servers fail at 24% timeout / 38% schema-drift rates — these are precisely our exposed surfaces.
stabilization_roi.case_against: Two honest concerns: (1) Pre-v1.0-mature products usually grow user base via visible features, not invisible reliability; if Fabric is still in 'find product-market fit' mode, a stability-only release risks looking dormant to a watching community. (2) The L0/L1/L2 protocol is so new (v1.6.0, ~10 days old) that it may itself have undiscovered design flaws — premature stabilization of a protocol that needs a v1.8 redesign is wasted work. Mitigation: don't stabilize protocol *semantics*, stabilize the *implementation surface* (writers, doctor, ledger, error handling, tests).
stabilization_roi.verdict: PROCEED with stabilization. The case_for is structurally stronger because Fabric's brand IS correctness, and the case_against's concerns are addressable by scoping (focus on implementation, not protocol redesign) and by treating the stabilization release as a *visible* event with a story (the version 1.7.1/1.8.0 narrative described in sequencing).
kpis_to_track: doctor_pass_rate_on_real_repos: % of `fabric doctor --strict` runs that exit 0 across 3 reference projects (cocos / next / vite). Target: 100% on green-path, single command to recover otherwise.
time_to_first_success: median minutes from `npm i -g @fenglimg/fabric-cli` to a working `fab_plan_context` call in Claude/Codex/Cursor. Target: <5min.
mcp_handshake_conformance: pass/fail count of golden-file fixture tests for tools/list, tools/call, resource read on README. Target: 100% in CI gate.
schema_drift_zero: count of CI failures from JSON Schema mismatch between schemas/fabric-config.json and packages/shared zod. Target: 0 between releases.

client_narrowing_impact.estimated_user_share: low — Windsurf (Codeium) had no Fabric-specific marketing; the README sentence is its only surface. No skills/hooks/snapshot tests imply no users actually validated end-to-end. Likely zero or near-zero active Fabric+Windsurf installs.
client_narrowing_impact.migration_story: Windsurf user runs `fab config install --clients cursor` to point Windsurf's user-config-manager at Fabric (Windsurf's MCP config format is identical JSON to Cursor's). README/migration-guide notes that Windsurf can still consume Fabric MCP via Cursor-format config — they just lose the auto-detection convenience.
client_narrowing_impact.rationale: Even though usage is likely zero, the *pattern* of how we drop a client matters for future scope decisions. Ship 1.7.1 with a deprecation warning in `fab doctor` when fabric.config.json has clientPaths.windsurf, plus README note. Remove writer/types/templates in 1.8.0. Total deprecation window: one minor (~2 weeks). Cheap insurance.

client_narrowing_impact.estimated_user_share: low — Roo Code is a smaller VS Code fork; Fabric template is a stub (templates/bootstrap/roo-fabric.md unreferenced by bootstrap-guide.ts at runtime). No skills/hooks. Likely zero active.
client_narrowing_impact.migration_story: Roo Code uses VS Code MCP wiring; user manually points Roo Code at the Fabric MCP server stdio command (the same one Cursor uses). README/migration-guide provides the JSON snippet. Roo's MCP support being VS Code-derivative means Cursor docs apply.
client_narrowing_impact.rationale: Same as Windsurf — uniformity in deprecation policy is itself a signal of maturity. One-minor deprecation, then remove.

client_narrowing_impact.estimated_user_share: low-to-medium — Gemini CLI has more name recognition than Windsurf/Roo, was specifically called out in CHANGELOG v1.1.0 migration notes ('MCP-capable AI client (Claude Code, Cursor with MCP, Codex, Gemini CLI)'), and i18n example strings still cite 'cursor,codex,gemini' as the canonical example trio. So it carries the highest perceived-support signal of the three.
client_narrowing_impact.migration_story: Two paths: (a) Gemini CLI users can keep using AGENTS.md directly (Gemini natively supports AGENTS.md per research.json finding #1) — Fabric's `.fabric/bootstrap/README.md` content can be pointed to manually. (b) For MCP-mediated rule retrieval, Gemini CLI's MCP wiring is JSON-format-compatible with Cursor/Claude; same migration as Windsurf/Roo. Migration guide should explicitly reframe: 'Gemini users can still benefit from Fabric's AGENTS.md protocol, just without first-class config emission.'
client_narrowing_impact.rationale: Gemini has the highest social-cost-of-removal of the three. Worth the explicit one-minor deprecation window with a doctor-emitted migration hint and a CHANGELOG explanation framing it as 'Fabric concentrates first-class support on three clients with end-to-end validated skills/hooks; Gemini is well-served via AGENTS.md directly.'

candidate_metrics: Test coverage % on critical paths (doctor, resolver, writers, MCP tools)
Doctor pass rate on real reference projects
Time-to-first-success for new user (init → working MCP)
GitHub issue close rate before next release
MCP handshake conformance test pass rate
Number of swallowed catches without log lines
MCP tool call median latency under stress
recommended_kpis: doctor_pass_rate_on_3_reference_projects (cocos/next/vite): 100% green or single-command-recover. This is the user-visible reliability proxy — when a maintainer runs `fabric doctor --strict` it should exit 0 or tell them exactly what to do. Measurable in CI via fixture projects.

time_to_first_success_under_5min: median time from `pnpm install` (or `npm i -g`) to a successful `fab_plan_context` response in any of the 3 supported clients. Captures install + init + serve + client-config + tool-call as one number. The single best leading indicator of perceived stability.

mcp_contract_test_coverage: 100% of MCP tools (plan_context, get_rule_sections, get_rules, append_intent if still public, update_registry) have golden-file JSON-Schema fixtures asserted in CI. This directly closes the #1 industry MCP failure category (38% schema drift).
perfection_criteria.rationale: Test coverage % is a vanity metric (you can have 95% coverage with no asserts). The three KPIs above are *outcome* metrics: doctor_pass_rate measures the user's lived reliability experience, time_to_first_success measures onboarding pain, and mcp_contract_test_coverage closes the single biggest documented failure mode. Each is automatable (CI runs reference-project doctor; CI times the e2e fixture; CI compares emitted schema to golden). All three can be reported in the README as evidence of stability.

must_do_before_release: Drop Windsurf/Roo/Gemini CLI from ClientKind union, json.ts writers, resolver, schema (15-file mechanical edit). Adds: deprecation warnings in fab doctor for legacy clientPaths fields, NOT immediate hard error.

Delete the 6 unreferenced legacy bootstrap templates (templates/bootstrap/{GEMINI,roo-fabric,windsurf-fabric}.md and the duplicate set under packages/cli/templates/bootstrap/) — confirmed unreachable from runtime per bootstrap-guide.ts.
Update README.md tagline (six → three clients), docs/contributing.md L10, docs/initialization.md L36+L441, docs/RULE_REGISTRY.md L90-92.
Refresh i18n strings in en.ts and zh-CN.ts (lines 40/51/54 EN, 39/49/52 zh-CN) — replace 'cursor,codex,gemini' example with 'claude,cursor,codex'. Refresh i18n.test.ts.snap.
Add client-scope contract test (vitest): assert ClientKind union members === ['ClaudeCodeCLI','ClaudeCodeDesktop','Cursor','CodexCLI']. Prevents accidental re-introduction.
Update config-install.test.ts L23-25 to assert 'cursor,codex' → {Cursor, CodexCLI}.
Author CHANGELOG entry for the release explicitly titled 'Client scope reduction + stabilization pass' with migration hints.
should_do_if_time: Replace _error.ts string-prefix matching with typed error classes (ApiPathEscapesError, NotFoundError, etc.) — closes a high-bug-magnet area.
Standardize event-ledger.ts on atomic-append semantics: tolerate trailing partial line on read; doctor --fix truncates to last complete record. Aligns with research.json fsyncgate guidance.
Add MCP handshake conformance test using @modelcontextprotocol/sdk client driver — closes #1 industry MCP failure mode.
Add snapshot tests per emitted client config (Claude × Cursor × Codex) × (project, user) scope = 6 fixtures.
Extend lefthook to run staged-file vitest + tsc --noEmit on touched packages.
Knip baseline run + fix top dead-code findings (research.json codebase_gaps).
Consolidate the two CLIENT_ALIASES tables (bootstrap.ts vs config.ts) into a single shared alias→ClientKind table.
Add MCP tool annotations (readOnlyHint/idempotentHint) to plan-context, rule-sections, get-rules tools (research.json best_practices).
wont_do_this_cycle: Refactor init.ts (1897 lines) into orchestrator + stages — high blast radius, no user-visible payoff in this cycle. Mark as v1.9 candidate after stability foundation lands.
Collapse the 5 JsonClientConfigWriter subclasses into a single table-driven writer — only 2 survive (Claude/Cursor) after the trim, so the consolidation savings shrink. Defer.
Add new MCP tools, new doctor checks beyond the 9 existing, or new dashboard views — explicitly counter to 'no new features' charter.
Introduce noUncheckedIndexedAccess + exactOptionalPropertyTypes — high typescript noise, 1-2 days of pure fixup. Worth doing but as its own focused minor (v1.8.x).
Mutation testing (Stryker) — premium tool, premature for current coverage levels. Revisit when KPI #1 (doctor_pass_rate) hits 100%.

1.7.1: Pure documentation + deprecation-warning patch: README/docs reduce client list to 3 in prose; doctor emits a soft warning when fabric.config.json contains clientPaths.{windsurf,rooCode,geminiCLI}; CHANGELOG announces deprecation timeline. NO code removal yet — TypeScript surface still has all 6 clients. Ship within 2-3 days. This buys the deprecation window without blocking the bigger 1.8.0 release.
1.8.0: Switch event-ledger to atomic semantics + doctor --fix recovery.
Replace _error.ts string-prefix matching with typed errors.
Single CHANGELOG entry framed as 'Stabilization release: scope reduction, error handling hardening, MCP conformance.' This is the visible 'we leveled up' release.
2.0.0: Reserved for the *next* protocol-level breaking change (e.g., a redesigned plan_context, a new rule-meta shape that breaks .fabric/agents.meta.json forward compat). Don't burn a major bump on client-scope reduction alone — that's a clean v1.8.0 minor with a deprecation warning predecessor. Saving the 2.0.0 slot signals to users that stability is now the default expectation.

risk_register.risk: User churn from feature stagnation perception
risk_register.impact: Pre-v1.0-stable user base is small and engaged with the protocol concept; they understand that 7 minors in 17 days is a *higher* signal of churn-risk than one focused stabilization release. As long as 1.8.0 ships with a visible CHANGELOG narrative ('we hardened X, Y, Z'), perception stays positive.
risk_register.mitigation: Frame 1.8.0 release notes as a *milestone*, not a holding pattern. Lead with the new KPIs (doctor_pass_rate, time_to_first_success). Add a 'Stability Commitment' paragraph in README.

risk_register.risk: Reputation backlash: 'they only support 3 clients now'
risk_register.impact: Real backlash requires an active user community for Windsurf/Roo/Gemini Fabric usage — which the codebase evidence (no skills, no hooks, no end-to-end tests) suggests does not exist. Risk is mostly social-media optics.
risk_register.mitigation: CHANGELOG and README explicitly frame this as 'first-class support concentrated on 3 clients with end-to-end validated skills/hooks/MCP, while Fabric's AGENTS.md protocol remains universally consumable.' The protocol remains open; only the convenience layer narrows.

risk_register.risk: Maintainer burnout from refactoring without visible features
risk_register.impact: Refactoring is genuinely less rewarding than feature work, especially solo-maintained. 1.8.0 scope must be tight enough to ship in ~1-2 weeks, not stretch.
risk_register.mitigation: Time-box: 1.7.1 in 2-3 days (pure docs + deprecation warning), 1.8.0 in 10-14 days (must-do list above). Defer aggressively to 'should_do_if_time' and 'wont_do_this_cycle' buckets. Ship 1.8.0 even if some should-do items slip — they become 1.8.1 patches.

risk_register.risk: Backward compat breakage with existing fabric.config.json files containing clientPaths.{windsurf,rooCode,geminiCLI}
risk_register.impact: If user upgrades from 1.7 to 1.8 with a config containing dropped fields, behavior could be: (a) zod validation error that blocks fab init (BAD), (b) silent ignore (acceptable), (c) doctor warning + auto-strip via --fix (BEST). Current zod schema is passthrough so accidental (a) is unlikely, but adding .strict() in the cleanup would trigger it.
risk_register.mitigation: Explicit decision: keep zod passthrough (or use .partial+.passthrough) so retired keys are silently ignored. Add a doctor check `client_scope_drift` that warns when retired keys are present and offers --fix to strip them. Document in migration guide.

risk_register.risk: Issue tracker noise: dropped-client users open complaints
risk_register.impact: Even if 0 active Windsurf+Fabric users exist today, the deprecation announcement could attract drive-by 'why not Windsurf' issues that consume maintainer attention.
risk_register.mitigation: Pre-write a canonical answer in docs/migration-1.8.md with the alternative-config recipe (Cursor JSON snippet works for Windsurf). Pin a GitHub issue 'Q: Why no Windsurf/Roo/Gemini first-class support?' with the migration recipe so future complaints close fast.

risk_register.risk: Stabilization debt missed in scope: a non-client-related stability bug ships in 1.8.0
risk_register.impact: 1.8.0 is framed as 'stable' — any post-release stability bug damages credibility more than usual.
risk_register.mitigation: Before tagging 1.8.0, run `fabric doctor --strict` against the 3 reference projects (cocos/next/vite) AND the werewolf-minigame-stub fixture. Add a release-blocking CI job that does this. Treat any failure as blocker.

risk_register.risk: Re-introduction of dropped clients via copy-paste in future PRs
risk_register.impact: Without a guardrail, a future contributor could reasonably re-add a Windsurf writer thinking it's harmless. Without contract test, CI passes.
risk_register.mitigation: Add the client-scope contract test in 1.8.0 (must_do_before_release item #5). Cost is ~20 lines of test code; benefit is permanent.

communication_plan.changelog_pattern: 1.7.1 entry: '### Deprecated' section listing Windsurf/Roo Code/Gemini CLI as 'first-class config emission deprecated; will be removed in 1.8.0.' Plus '### Documentation' section pointing to migration guide. 1.8.0 entry: '### Removed' section listing the 3 clients with one-line migration recipe each, '### Changed' framing the release as 'Stabilization milestone,' and a new top-level '### Stability KPIs' section reporting on doctor_pass_rate, time_to_first_success, mcp_contract_test_coverage. This last is novel for the project but fits the brand promise of consensus/correctness.
communication_plan.doctor_messaging_changes: Add three new doctor codes: (1) `legacy_client_path_present` — manual or fixable, fires when fabric.config.json contains clientPaths.{windsurf,rooCode,geminiCLI}; --fix strips the field. (2) `client_scope_outside_first_class` — manual, fires when --clients=… includes any of the 3 retired aliases; suggests the migration recipe. (3) `bootstrap_template_legacy` — manual, fires if templates/bootstrap/{GEMINI,roo-fabric,windsurf-fabric}.md still exist in user's repo (they shouldn't, but defensive). All three should produce HUMAN-READABLE messages, not just codes — first-time users seeing 'legacy_client_path_present' should immediately know what to do.
communication_plan.readme_updates: (1) Line 11 sentence: 'Fabric v1.8.0 is an MCP-first AGENTS.md protocol with first-class support for Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex CLI. The protocol itself is client-agnostic — any AGENTS.md-aware AI tool can read Fabric-managed rules.' (2) Add a new 'Supported Clients' section after Architecture, explaining the distinction between first-class (skills/hooks/auto-config) and protocol-compatible (read AGENTS.md). (3) Add a 'Stability' section near the bottom showing the 3 KPIs with current values from CI. The KPI reporting is itself a marketing moat — few open-source CLI tools publish stability metrics.

questions_for_user: Confirm the 1.7.1 → 1.8.0 two-step is acceptable, vs a single 1.8.0 with `Removed` semver-correct breakage. The two-step buys ~2 weeks deprecation window for the (likely zero) affected users; cost is one extra release tag.

Confirm the recommended KPI trio (doctor_pass_rate / time_to_first_success / mcp_contract_test_coverage) — these become public commitments. Are there others the maintainer wants to track privately but not publish?
fabric.config.json compat: confirm we go with 'silent ignore + doctor warning + --fix auto-strip' for retired clientPaths fields. Alternative is hard zod error which is simpler but breaks upgrades.

Reference projects for doctor_pass_rate KPI: cocos / next / vite are the existing forensic variants. Is there a real-world repo (e.g., the werewolf-minigame-stub or an external partner repo) we should add as a 4th gate? Real-world variance catches more bugs than synthetic fixtures.

1.8.0 release narrative: should we publish a short blog/README post explicitly titled 'Why Fabric is concentrating on 3 clients,' or let the CHANGELOG speak for itself? The former is more proactive comms; the latter is less work.
